1.headline: Build reliable junior Salesforce fundamentals.
1.summary: Focus on CRM fundamentals, Admin configuration, Flow, Apex basics, SOQL, LWC basics, security basics, and PD1 foundation.

topics.topic: CRM, Objects, Fields, and Record Pages
topics.reason: Junior developers must understand platform configuration before writing code.

topics.topic: Object, Field, and Record Security Basics
topics.reason: Interviewers expect CRUD, FLS, OWD, roles, profiles, and permission sets.
Modern Salesforce teams expect developers to know when Flow is better than Apex.

topics.topic: Apex Syntax, Collections, and Governor Limits
topics.reason: Core junior developer screening usually starts with limits, lists, sets, maps, and basic classes.
SOQL is required for almost every Apex and debugging task.
Bulk-safe trigger patterns separate production-ready juniors from memorized answers.

topics.topic: LWC Basics, Events, and Wire Service
topics.reason: Most developer roles ask at least basic LWC component questions.

2.headline: Move from fundamentals to production-ready delivery.
2.summary: Strengthen trigger frameworks, Flow handoff decisions, LWC data access, testing, deployment basics, and PD1 certification readiness.

topics.reason: A 2-year developer should discuss maintainable trigger structure, not only syntax.

topics.topic: Apex Testing and Test Data Strategy
topics.reason: Testing depth is a common separator for real project experience.

topics.topic: LWC with Apex, LDS, and Parent-Child Events
topics.reason: Most junior-to-mid roles expect practical LWC communication patterns.
Interviewers want to know when you avoid code.
Scenario answers prove you can apply concepts beyond definitions.

3.headline: Become a confident independent Salesforce Developer.
3.summary: Own Apex, LWC, SOQL, integrations, async processing, deployment, and real debugging stories.

topics.reason: Three-year roles expect reusable services, selectors, and safer limit handling.
Integration basics are common for mid-level Salesforce interviews.

topics.topic: Future, Queueable, Batch, and Scheduled Apex
topics.reason: Async choice and limits are repeated interview themes.
Mid-level roles expect data refresh, error handling, and performance awareness.
Security review mindset matters for production code.

4.headline: Deepen delivery ownership and cross-cloud thinking.
4.summary: Strengthen integration patterns, Platform Events, advanced Flow, sharing, deployment discipline, and production support stories.

topics.reason: Four-year candidates must explain retries, idempotency, auth, and observability.

topics.topic: Platform Events and Change Data Capture
topics.reason: Event-driven answers show modern architecture awareness.

topics.topic: Advanced Sharing and User-Mode Data Access
topics.reason: Mid-level developers must protect data across Apex, SOQL, and UI.
Reusable, testable code becomes expected at this level.

5.headline: Operate like a senior engineer with architecture judgment.
5.summary: Prioritize scalable Apex, LWC performance, integration architecture, CI/CD, production debugging, code review, and release impact analysis.

topics.reason: Senior roles require explaining CPU, heap, query selectivity, and bulk patterns under load.
Senior frontend answers include state, cache, refresh, accessibility, and component boundaries.
Senior developers should design auth, retries, dead-letter handling, and monitoring.
Architecture scenario answers prove readiness for senior interviews.

6.headline: Lead delivery, design decisions, and team quality.
6.summary: Blend architecture, mentoring, CI/CD, code governance, integration ownership, technical debt control, and stakeholder communication.

topics.reason: Lead developers must define patterns other developers can follow.
Technical leads own design tradeoffs and release safety.
Lead roles require proactively preventing data and compliance risk.
Leadership interviews test conflict, mentoring, ownership, and risk communication.

7.headline: Lead platform architecture and enterprise integrations.
7.summary: Focus on integration architecture, data model governance, LDV, event-driven design, AI readiness, release readiness, and cross-team decisions.

topics.reason: Technical leads must select patterns across APIs, middleware, events, and batch loads.
Modern Salesforce orgs use events for decoupling and scale.
AI and personalization strategies increasingly depend on Data Cloud.
Leads must understand agent topics, actions, grounding, and trust boundaries.

8.headline: Prepare for architect-level Salesforce and AI/Data Cloud discussions.
8.summary: Prioritize cross-cloud architecture, Data Cloud, Agentforce, Trust Layer, identity, integration strategy, governance, and executive-level tradeoffs.

topics.reason: Architect-ready candidates must explain DLO, DMO, identity resolution, and activation.
Agent architecture is now a senior/architect differentiator.
AI answers must include security, auditability, and data minimization.
Architecture interviews test end-to-end tradeoffs, not isolated facts.

9.headline: Own platform strategy, governance, and high-impact architecture.
9.summary: Focus on platform governance, scale, release adoption, AI/data strategy, org consolidation, integration portfolios, and measurable business outcomes.

topics.reason: Principal candidates must set guardrails across teams and releases.
Data strategy is central to AI and customer 360 programs.
Principal roles require safe automation strategy, not simple feature usage.
Governance without security depth is not credible at principal level.

10.headline: Lead enterprise transformation with Salesforce, data, and AI.
10.summary: Prioritize business-aligned architecture, enterprise migration strategy, AI governance, Data Cloud, integration portfolios, platform operating model, and release strategy.

topics.reason: Ten-year candidates need structured architecture narratives and executive clarity.
Enterprise AI must connect safely to source systems and business processes.
Architects must define control models, not only implementation details.
Architecture interviews heavily test influence and decision framing.
